preFix.title: Making a skein on a niddy-noddy
preFix.subtitle: Winding finished handspun yarn off a bobbin and onto a niddy-noddy to form a measured skein
preFix.excerpt: The niddy-noddy winds handspun yarn into a measured skein for washing, dyeing, or storage. Count the circuits to calculate yardage, tie in four places, and soak to set the twist and bloom the yarn.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from general handspinning finishing references including Judith MacKenzie and Abby Franquemont's works, and traditional skein-making practice documentation.

content.text: After spinning and plying, handspun yarn on a bobbin needs to come off into a
skein
for washing, dyeing, storage, or measurement. The
niddy-noddy

is the traditional tool for this: a central shaft with two T-shaped crossbars offset at 90 degrees. Winding the yarn in figure-8 loops around both crossbars creates an even coil where each circuit corresponds to a fixed measured length: typically 2 metres for a full-size niddy-noddy or approximately 1 yard for a shorter one. Count the circuits as you wind and you have the total yardage of the finished skein. The final step of soaking and hanging with a small weight completes
twist setting
and produces
blooming
in fine-fibre yarns.

items.name: Bobbin or cop of finished handspun yarn
Niddy-noddy (2 m circuit or 1 yd circuit)
Soft measuring tape (to verify niddy-noddy circuit length)
Swift (to hold a cop while winding)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Measure the niddy-noddy circuit

Before winding, confirm the circuit length of the niddy-noddy so the wrap count translates accurately to yardage. Tie a length of cotton to one arm, run it around the full figure-8 path (down the shaft, around one crossbar end, back up the shaft, around the opposite crossbar end, and back to the start), and measure the cotton. Record this number. Most full-size niddy-noddies measure between 180 cm and 210 cm per circuit.
Step 2: Secure the yarn end and begin winding

Tie the yarn end to the shaft of the niddy-noddy with a loose overhand knot, leaving a 10 cm tail. Hold the shaft in one hand and pass the yarn over one crossbar arm, down the shaft, under and around the opposite crossbar arm, and back up. This completes one circuit. Keep even tension as you wind: too tight distorts the skein and makes it difficult to remove; too slack lets the loops slide off the crossbars.
Step 3: Count wraps and calculate yardage
Count each complete circuit as you wind. If the niddy-noddy measures 200 cm per circuit and you wind 50 circuits, the skein contains 100 metres of yarn. If
WPI
measurement is also needed, take it directly from the bobbin before winding: wrap the yarn around a ruler without stretching and count how many wraps fill 2.5 cm (1 inch).
Step 4: Tie the skein in four places

Before removing the skein from the niddy-noddy, tie it in four evenly spaced places with short lengths of cotton waste yarn. Use a figure-8 tie (pass one end under the skein, cross over the top, and tie the ends together above): a figure-8 tie holds the skein loops in position without cinching them tight. Plain ties can trap and distort fibres. The cotton ties also help keep sections distinct for dyeing.
Step 5: Remove from the niddy-noddy

Slide one crossbar arm through the skein loops, then ease the loops off the other crossbar. Twist the skein loosely on itself two or three times and pass one end of the twist through the other to form a self-securing bundle for storage. For immediate washing, keep it as a loose skein.
Step 6: Wash and set the twist

Soak the skein in warm water (30 to 40 °C) for 20 minutes to allow the fibre to fully absorb the water. Add a small amount of wool wash if the yarn was not scoured during spinning. Lift out, squeeze gently without wringing, and roll in a clean towel to remove excess water. Hang the skein with a small weight (a wooden spoon or similar) to pull it straight while drying. The water allows the twist memory of the fibre to relax into the balanced state produced by plying; as it dries, the skein holds that shape permanently.

items.fix: Use four figure-8 ties spaced evenly around the skein before removing it. Check that each tie goes through all loops, not just part of the skein.
items.cause: Ties placed incorrectly or not enough ties used
items.problem: Skein collapses into a tangled mess when removed from the niddy-noddy

items.fix: Wind at a steady pace with consistent hand pressure. If a section looks tighter, re-wind that section before tying.
items.problem: Skein loops stretch unevenly when hanging

items.fix: Soak for 20 full minutes, not less. Use a slightly heavier weight during drying, or snap the skein gently after lifting it from the water to help the loops settle.
items.cause: Insufficient soak time or weight too light during drying
items.problem: Yarn still feels kinked and coiled after washing

items.fix: Use a stitch counter clipped to the niddy-noddy, or make a physical mark with a short waste yarn after every 10 circuits.
items.cause: Losing track during long winding sessions
items.problem: Cannot count wraps accurately while winding
